speaker: interviewer
text: This is July the 24th, 1996. We're interviewing Rachel Moses. And the interviewer is Barbara Linz. The interview is taking place in Sydney, Australia. And the language is English. It's July the 24th, 1996. My name is Barbara Linz, L-I-N-Z. And I'm interviewing Rachel Moses, in Sydney, New South Wales, Australia. And the language is English. Can you tell me your name, and how you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My name is Rachel Moses. R-A-C-H-E-L M-O-S-E-S.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wircer, W-I-R-C-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any nicknames?

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- [? Rachela. ?] [LAUGHS] Well, that's not important.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: The 26th of August, '27.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old are you now?

speaker: interviewee
text: 68, nearly on-- going on 69, old, old lady, [LAUGHS] very old. I don't feel old. But I am old. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Which city and which country were you born in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm born in Poland. And it's a small city of 12,000 inhabitants. It's called Bilgoraj. B--

speaker: interviewer
text: And how would you spell it?

speaker: interviewee
text: B-- Shall I spell it? B-I-L-G-O-R-A-J.

speaker: interviewer
text: Near which big city is Bilgoraj?

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, it's not far from Lublin, say, two or three hours traveling by a car. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me about Bilgoraj?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Well, I was a 12-year-old girl when the war started. So many memories I haven't got. But somehow they're coming back now, after reading the book of my city, which I came across-- I have it in my possession for probably 25 years. But I've never read it. Because it's in Yiddish. And I've just come across it the other day. And I decided to struggle through it. And I read it. So lots of memories came back because of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me about your early childhood. How many Jews were there in Bilgoraj?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Well, from 12,000 people, 65% were Jewish. We lived a very comfortable life. My father was a bank manager in one of the banks in Bilgoraj.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yitzak, Isaac. He actually wasn't born in Bilgoraj. He came from little township also not far from Bilgoraj, which is called [INAUDIBLE]. My grandparents lived there. He had, I think 1, 2, 3 brothers, no sisters. And when he was introduced by [INAUDIBLE] to my mother, my mother was only 16 years old when she she got sort of semi-engaged to him. But then he went away. He went to study. He went to Warsaw, and he finished an accountancy course, which at that time not every Jew could have done it. But he did it. And he came back. After that, they got married. And he, of course, he lived in Bilgoraj with us. At that time the girls did not go away from home, especially my mother came from a very wealthy family in Bilgoraj. And then my grandfather, of course, wanted her to stay in that city.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father's family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was a traditional Jew, religious, but not-- Orthodox, but not-- [NON-ENGLISH] [LAUGHS] religious. He never had any payot. He would wear a hat. At home, we had a kosher home. And-- and so my life was very colorful when I was a young girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your mother's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had every comfort at home. We always had somebody at home which looked after us. Because my mother, she was really a lady. And so I never-- never had any problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your-- was your mother's family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. My grandfather was a Hasid. And whenever his rabbi used to come to the city, he used to stay in my grandfather's home. And he had a big home, my grandfather. So he used to take cooks, and that made big for a whole week, they used to celebrate. And, you know, they used to have a very good time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was his rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]. I don't know. [INAUDIBLE], I've wroten to somewhere down, but it's not important. And my mother had 1, 2, 3 sisters, and three brothers. Unfortunately only two sisters perished during the war. And my grandfather also, he married-- my grandmother actually, the name I'm called-- she died very young. Because there was at that time an epidemic of cholera. And she caught cholera epidemic. And then he remarried again. And had one son of the second marriage. And just before the war maybe, '37 or '36, he married again. He was still a young man. And-- and I mean in those days, he wasn't young. In my opinion, he wasn't young. But now thinking about it, he was young. He married again. There was this lady, this auntie. He had three more little children, and they all perished during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rappaport. My mother's maiden name is Rappaport. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you close to him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much. I was his oldest grandchild. He loved me. I used to run every Shabbat to him, and sit on his knees. And he was-- he really loved me. I used to go to school. And he had a big store. And he-- they were selling all these school books, and everything actually. I used to pop in before I went to school, always gave me 10 [? groschen. ?] That was big money. And I used to pop in occasionally to his home. And I really loved to go there. Because I know I was very welcome. And for Purim we used to dress up, and used to go there, and taking [NON-ENGLISH] to each other. And it was a lovely life. I mean I can't complain. I haven't suffered. Anti-Semitism was, but I didn't feel it. Put it this way. Because I was going to school. And I had quite a few Christian girlfriends, and used to go to their homes. They used to come to my home. We used to make like a schoolwork together. And they all were from very good families. And I was always welcome there. So I really-- I didn't felt much anti-Semitism at that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What school did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I went to a public school. And a part of it, there was a Hebrew school, which was called [? Yavna, ?] and which my father, he was the one who actually opened it. And he was the grand--

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father. And he used to bring-- I remember-- he brought a teacher from Lithuania. And on his own-- on his account. And he stayed with us a few weeks in our house. And then we settled him somewhere in a home. And he used to teach us Hebrew. And then it's become a very big school. And after-- like in Poland, we used to go till 1 o'clock, we went 8:00-- from 8 o'clock to 1 o'clock to the public school, and came home for lunch. And after lunch we used to go every day to that [? Yavna ?] school to learn Hebrew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if I got a little bit of Hebrew left, it's from that time which I learned. What was the ideology of that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH]. This was Zionistic. Because my father was affiliated a lot with the Zionism. He was always dreaming one day to go to Israel. And a matter of fact, he helped one of his brothers just before the war, he send him away. And he survived the war during-- I mean, in Israel, he died since. But-- but because of him, they send him out. It was, I think, in '38 or '37, he managed to escape from Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that quite unusual to be so Zionistic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well-- well-- well, at that time, just in the '30s, the [NON-ENGLISH] start, the movements of the [NON-ENGLISH] start to be very prominent. And-- and so they used to send them on a preparatory [NON-ENGLISH] and [NON-ENGLISH] in Poland to prepare themselves to go to Israel. And I-- I think that was semi-legal that he went to Israel, and settled. And he was quite happy. Happy, I mean, you know they were struggling a lot at that time in Israel. But he survived the war. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: What about your siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I have got a brother-- I had-- I mean, I still got him. Since then, I've got another little brother. I've got Smilik, and my brother, and I've got a sister, Carmela, which they live in Israel. And I've got also younger brother, Yosi, which was born after the war, just a year before my father died. And my father was 53, and my mom was 49. She never knew that she was pregnant until she realized that she was, and it was too late to do anything. A matter of fact, I was pregnant at the time with Gilla, and I prepared all her documents to come to Rome. At the time we lived in Rome. And one day Smilik comes home from work. And he always-- good news or bad news, always very gently put to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after lunch, he stands behind me, and says to me. Mama is not coming. I said, what do you mean she's not coming? We've got the documents ready for her. We've got the passport ready. We've got the ticket. What do you mean she's not coming? Well, Mama is expecting a baby. Well, I will never forget it. I said, mama is expecting a baby? How could she do that to me? [LAUGHS] Well, she-- that was her actually savior. Because after that, my father-- after nine-- nine months Yosi was when he died. And really, that gave her-- because she adored-- my father adored her. You know, nothing was good enough for to give to her. So of course, she had a very good sheltered life during the war, and-- and before the war. He always sheltered her, sheltered all of us really. He was a fantastic father, and husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- you know, so in that Yosi gave her that sense to live. And-- and he is now-- he's got a very good position. And he's grown up a very lovely young man, married with three beautiful children. Matter of fact, we were there a couple of years ago on the bat mitzvah of his daughter. And it was really very nice to be with all my family-- I mean actually, my two brothers and sister live in Israel. And they're very happy there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Going back to Bilgoraj, were your-- were your parents and your father involved in the community at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, as I said, he was working in the bank, which is a-- it was actually a Jewish bank, cooperative workers. And apart of that, he was chosen in the municipality of Bilgoraj with three other Jews and it was the fourth. Which at that time, just before the war, it wasn't easy. Because, as you know very well, and he was helping very much lots of people who were deprived of-- You know, because they're the poor, they were more beaten, and-- and took-- take away-- took away their livelihood sort of to say. And he was helping whatever he could. Because he had lots of friends, no Jewish friends. The burgermeister was a friend of his. And whenever somebody needed a favor, Yitzak Wircer went, you know, was succeed to do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it mainly Jews that he helped or non-Jews as well?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mainly Jews, because non-Jews didn't need his help, put it this way. Because I remember, in fact, there was a family. And they were selling newspapers. And their daughter used to be my teacher. She used to teach me. My mother thought I'm brilliant. So she said I don't have to go. Because school started in the home, when you were seven years old.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she decided, no. She-- when I was five and a half, she took this lady which was the daughter of this newspaper fellow. And she decided to take lessons, private lessons. So-- so I actually never went to first class. I went right away to the second class.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- they-- they-- they had a stand where they were selling newspapers. And just before the war, I think '38, Mom was tell me-- I can't remember exactly. They took away their rights for them to sell. So my father went and intervened. And they were able-- otherwise they would have starved. They wouldn't have had no more to live. So that's one of the examples what he did. But he did a lot of good, he really was his--

speaker: interviewer
text: I still remember, in fact, when there's a Polish national holiday, like the 3rd of May. Right? So the Jewish children used to go to the synagogue. And the Christians used to go to the church. And there used to be celebrations and speeches. And my father always, he was on the [INAUDIBLE]. He would stand up, and I was so proud of him that he talks to the whole school. That's my father. It was something, because looking always towards it. Was that in the-- in the [? Yavna ?] school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That was-- No, no. That was from the-- from the public school. He was chosen to speak. He was a very good speaker. And so I was very proud of him. Because he did a lot of things for us during the war. Because of him, we really survived this. He helped us in every way, always with a smile and a joke. And he was a wonderful fellow.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language did you speak at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home we spoke Polish and Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: And out of home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly Polish, mostly Polish with my friends I spoke.

speaker: interviewer
text: And school? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: At school, Polish. Polish, Yiddish too, but my Yiddish was atrocious. But mixed at-- to Mom and Dad, to Grandfather I used to speak Yiddish. But normally, I speak Polish and Yiddish both, mixture. I acquired so many languages during my life, [NON-ENGLISH], I can go and talk about it. But we'll go further, and I'll come to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your school boys and girls?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. This was a combined school, yeah. The [? Yavna ?] school the same. It was a good school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the relationship between the Jews and the non-Jews in Bilgoraj?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I-- I really can't remember so far. I mean being there, I never had any problems. I really and truly I can't say that I had-- that anybody told me-- you [SPEAKING POLISH] that is as-- that's an expression, a Polish expression that is horrible Jewish girl or something like that. I really never had any of that problem. I don't know why. But I never had any of that-- any problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as I said before, I used to go to all the Jewish-- the non-Jewish homes, and I was always welcomed. A matter of fact, at Christmas time, we never had a tree. I used to go to my girlfriends. And we used to sit down by the tree, and singing the carols, the Christmas carols. I used to love it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I still remember, a matter of fact one day, I might have been six or seven or eight years old. Our maid took-- took me because our house was opposite the church, my church in Bilgoraj. And she used to take me to-- to-- to the Mass, Christmas Mass. And my mother was very upset. She was really upset with her, why she did. And I was so happy. I was-- I even kneeled. You know, this was something new for me. And I was really very happy to do that. I used to sing all the Christmas carols, and I loved it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your-- your grandfather who was very religious, did he object to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so. I've never told him. I mean, he would have never known about it. Because I shouldn't have-- you know, I shouldn't have gone to a church. This was something which-- that was outrageous.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did he object to your father's Zionism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A matter of fact, there's a few years that they never sort of spoke to each other, because as I said, my father brought this teacher from Rovna. And that was really a Zionist sort of school. It wasn't a Zionist-- it was a Mizrahi school. But [NON-ENGLISH], that was associated with Zionism, the whole. And he was going to-- so he wasn't very happy about it. But then they made-- they made peace just before the war. He was-- otherwise he was-- I don't know what else to tell you. Ask me another question.

speaker: interviewer
text: In-- in those years, when-- after Hitler's rise to power, did your parents talk about it at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I mean, as far as I remember, I remember that we used to listen to the news. And not many people had radios. You-- it might have been for you, something new. But not many people had a radio in their home. And I remember we had a big telephone and radio. And there's lots of people at night which used to come to our house. And my father-- my parents used to put a radio in the-- in the window. So we used to live in a home-- in a house where this was a porch-- yeah-- a courtyard. Right? So he used to put the radio into the window. And there might be a dozen or two dozen people who were standing around and listening to the news. But I didn't take any interest. I don't-- you know, I don't know. I just didn't-- I can't remember really whether I was interested in what was going on. This-- when we start really of worrying about it, it was 1939, when we went to Zaklikow. That was a-- a place where every year we went on holidays. Like when the school break at the 29th of June until the end of August was school holidays. So my parents used to rent a house in a part-- it was actually not very far from Lublin. And we used to travel there by horse and buggy, [LAUGHS] you know, took everything with us, dishes. Because we used to have a kosher home, and we used to cook there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in '39, my father was called back from our holidays. They rang up from the bank. Wircer comes right away back, and they start talking about it. And then later we came home. And then I started to be interested in what was going on. But before that, I didn't even know that. Did your-- did you ever hear your father talk of leaving the country or securing assets?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. He never talked about it. Because lots of people who-- who left-- who left Bilgoraj, people who could not live comfortable. We were comfortable off. And [DRINKS] we never thought of it that we'll have to leave Bilgoraj. We were forced later to leave. But we-- we never thought of it that we'll make a life somewhere else. Because we were very comfortable. And my father had his own sort of profession. And we were children going to school. He was dreaming for me to go to university, you know. But of course that never came to it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went to school from-- what year to what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: From-- well till '30-- from-- from-- from '39. We come back. It was six years.

speaker: interviewee
text: So '33, it's yes, we went to school. And-- and then-- I would-- if the war wouldn't have started, then most people would have gone to gymnasium. But the war started, so-- I know that I already was inscribed to go. And just before the war, it opened a gymnasium in our city. Because before there was no gymnasium. It was a small city. But unfortunately, I never arrived there because of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened? Tell me about when the war started.

speaker: interviewee
text: When-- when the war started, as everybody knows, it was the 1st of September 1939. And-- and lots of people start arriving from Krakow and Warsaw, from that part of Poland, running through our city. I don't know. It was-- it must have been a strategic point. That Bilgoraj, although it was a small city, but lots and lots of people came through our city. And they were talking what's happening, you know, they hardly had any things with them. And I remember my mother used to stay outside with teas and sandwiches and cakes. And they used to put some tables, and give to people. Because they couldn't stay there. I don't know where they were running. But they were running through our city. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anyone ever stay with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. A family from Germany, funny enough, my father was also an agent for Lloyd's Insurance. And one family from Germany run away, knew my father. And they came to our city. And we put them up in our house. And my parents gave them away their bedroom. They didn't sleep in their beds. And they stayed with us for a couple of nights, and then they went further. And about eight weeks after the-- eight days after the war started, mainly the 8th of September. It was Friday morning. And my father came in and said to Mom, you know, I want to go to the city and buy a rucksack. Because we might have to run away from home, and we won't be able to carry much with us, as we saw the other people had hardly anything with them. So-- and the bank was just-- on the-- like not far, just around the corner from our house. And so he said, don't wait for me for lunch. Because normally we used to have lunch at 3 o'clock. I'll be back whenever I can. That was just before lunch. And then my little sister went out with a little girlfriend to get something. And my little brother was outside playing with a friend of his. All of a sudden, a lady, her name was Mrs. [INAUDIBLE]. They had a big store in front of our house. And she came into the courtyard. They had a magazine there, which they kept all their goods. And she calls my mom over. It was about 3 o'clock in the afternoon, when we were supposed to sit down and have lunch. And says, Mrs. Wircer, I want to talk to you. So-- and-- and I wasn't at home either. Because I went to visit a girlfriend of mine, which also they had a-- a place, also they had a house in the same courtyard. So it was a courtyard, but it had five different inhabitants. And my little brother, when I heard talking with the lady that was asking my mom to come along, of course, he ran around with her. Because he thought they might find some little bits and pieces in that magazine. And she started talking to Mom. She said, look, they were talking about they're going to bombard Bilgoraj. I mean, as soon as she pronounced that word, a bomb fall into our kitchen. That was the first bomb which really fall into our city. And it was just, I think, a miracle. Because 3 o'clock, we would have sit, five of us at the dinner table, having lunch. It made a big hole in our kitchen. And the whole city came running afterwards looking what happened. And the burgermeister came, and everybody they could not believe it. That a family of five, 3 o'clock in the afternoon survived something like that. And, of course, we didn't have anymore where to sleep. So we went to stay with my grandfather for a few days.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did he stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- to stay at his house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did he stay near you, near you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, not far. Whole of Bilgoraj wasn't a big, big place, so not far. So we stayed with him for a few days. And then in our city, actually out of the city was living a Mr. Miller. He was of German origin. But they would start talking that he is a German spy. And somehow managed to-- they managed to put arson around all Bilgoraj. And all of a sudden, Bilgoraj started burning, after-- 10 days after the war started. And we all started running to the river. Because that's only way that we thought we might survive. And when we arrived there, my father remembered that the Sefer Torah was hidden in our place. So he wrapped a blanket, and my mother was begging him, please don't go. Because Bilgoraj was burning. You couldn't-- but he wouldn't listen. He says, I'm not going to let the Sefer Torah burning. I'm just not going to. If I can save it, I will. And he went, and he survived. He brought the Sefer Torah back to us. He was all black. I still remember him, like distinctly now, when I'm looking at him, all black. The whole-- and he had-- he-- he managed to wet the blanket. So the fire wouldn't-- the burning would go around him. And-- and then in we stayed for one night at the river. And in the morning, the German planes started circling. And they saw us running. So they start shooting, I mean from the-- from the planes, and lots of people died.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll break there for the end of the tape.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
